coredata.dc:title: Dual-channel fluorescence detection of mercuric (II) and glutathione by down- and up-conversion fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Zhangjun
Yan, Fanyong
Xu, Jinxia
Zhang, Jin
Wei, Junfu
Luo, Yunmei
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with high fluorescent quantum yield (φf = 62%) and down- and up-conversion fluorescence properties were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane. The CDs displayed the capability to absorb excitation wavelength at 660 nm and 330 nm with fluorescence emission wavelength at 398 nm and 399 nm, respectively. The CDs showed high selectivity towards Hg2+ against various metal ions. Around 70% fluorescence was quenched by 40 μM Hg2+ through dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. Because of stronger affinity between the thiol and Hg2+, over 90% fluorescence was recovered by adding 40 μM glutathione to CDs-Hg2+ system. The calibration curves exhibited wide linear region for Hg2+ (0–4 μM) and glutathione (0–30 μM). The limits of detection with down- and up-conversion for Hg2+ were calculated to be 0.23 μM and 0.25 μM, and for glutathione were 0.28 μM and 0.29 μM, respectively. Inspired by the sensing results, logic gates with Hg2+ and glutathione as inputs were also established. Most importantly, this method was applied to detect Hg2+ and glutathione in tap water and lake water, and the recovery values were obtained to be 96.2%–110.4% and 93.4%–96.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Down- and up-conversion fluorescence
Mercuric (II)
Glutathione
Logic gate
Real water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The CDs With Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence Detection of Hg2+ and GSH
Fluorescence Detection of Hg2+ by Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence
Fluorescence Detection of GSH by Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence
Determination of Hg2+ and GSH in Practical Water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: high-resolution TEM image). (b) Particle size distribution of the CDs. (c) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs, the inset shows photographs of the CDs under daylight (left) and under UV light (right) in aqueous solution.

(a) Emission spectra of the CDs (excitation wavelength from 270 to 370 nm). (b) Emission spectra of the CDs (excitation wavelength from 600 to 700 nm). (c) CIE color coordinates of the CDs (excitation wavelength from 270 to 370 nm). (d) CIE color coordinates of the CDs (excitation wavelength from 600 to 700 nm).
(a) Fluorescence intensities of the CDs at different pH. (b) Fluorescence intensities of the CDs at different NaCl solution. (c) Tyndall effect of CDs in aqueous solution.

(a) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs upon the addition of various 40 μM metal ions. (b) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 660 nm) of the CDs upon the addition of various 40 μM metal ions. (c) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+, inset: the linear region of F0-F plot with the Hg2+ concentration from 0 μM to 40 μM (λex = 330 nm). (d) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 660 nm) of the CDs system upon the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+, inset: the linear region of F0-F plot with the Hg2+ concentration from 0 μM to 40 μM (λex = 660 nm). (e) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs upon the addition of 40 μM various metal ions without and with 40 μM Hg2+. (f) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 660 nm) of the CDs upon the addition of 40 μM various metal ions without and with 40 μM Hg2+.

(a) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs-Hg2+ system upon the addition of different concentrations of GSH, inset: the linear region of F0-F plot with the GSH concentration from 0 μM to 30 μM (λex = 330 nm). (b) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 660 nm) of the CDs-Hg2+ system upon the addition of different concentrations of GSH, inset: the linear region of F0-F plot with the GSH concentration from 0 μM to 30 μM (λex = 660 nm). (c) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs-Hg2+ system to 40 μM various amino acids. (d) Fluorescence spectra (λex = 660 nm) of the CDs-Hg2+ system to 40 μM various amino acids.

(a) Stern-Volmer plot of down-conversion for the quenching of CDs fluorescence at various concentrations of Hg2+. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs before and after adding different concentrations of Hg2+.
Schematic illustration of logic gates presented for CDs/Hg2+/GSH.
Schematic illustration of CDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+and GSH through down- and up-conversion fluorescence.
Detection of Hg2+ spiked in tap water and real lake water samples.
Detection of GSH spiked in tap water and real lake water samples.
Comparison of different CDs-based Hg2+ and GSH fluorescence probe.

head.ce:title: Dual-channel fluorescence detection of mercuric (II) and glutathione by down- and up-conversion fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Joint International Research Laboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical University, Zunyi 563003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Joint International Research Laboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo 0010024, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with high fluorescent quantum yield (φf = 62%) and down- and up-conversion fluorescence properties were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane. The CDs displayed the capability to absorb excitation wavelength at 660 nm and 330 nm with fluorescence emission wavelength at 398 nm and 399 nm, respectively. The CDs showed high selectivity towards Hg2+ against various metal ions. Around 70% fluorescence was quenched by 40 μM Hg2+ through dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. Because of stronger affinity between the thiol and Hg2+, over 90% fluorescence was recovered by adding 40 μM glutathione to CDs-Hg2+ system. The calibration curves exhibited wide linear region for Hg2+ (0–4 μM) and glutathione (0–30 μM). The limits of detection with down- and up-conversion for Hg2+ were calculated to be 0.23 μM and 0.25 μM, and for glutathione were 0.28 μM and 0.29 μM, respectively. Inspired by the sensing results, logic gates with Hg2+ and glutathione as inputs were also established. Most importantly, this method was applied to detect Hg2+ and glutathione in tap water and lake water, and the recovery values were obtained to be 96.2%–110.4% and 93.4%–96.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles [1, 2], have attracted considerable interests due to their prominent properties in terms of chemical inertness, easy surface modification, nontoxicity, photostability and biocompatibility [3, 4]. Based on these significant advantages, CDs have made great progress in a variety of applications, such as chemical sensors [5, 6], optoelectronic devices [7, 8] and biomedicine [9–11]. Besides, the CDs also have a fascinating performance, up-conversion fluorescence emission. Up-conversion is a nonlinear, anti-stokes process of combining two or more low energy near-infrared photons to obtain the emission of a high energy [12–14]. Because of the unique anti-stokes photoluminescence properties, up-conversion fluorescent materials have special advantages in luminescence devices, anti-counterfeiting, biomedical diagnostics and so on. [15–18]

Currently, the majority of reported up-conversion fluorescent materials include inorganic crystals doped with rare-earth metals, cocooned organic dyes and quantum dots in shells [19–23]. However, the potential toxicity and environmental hazards limited their practical applications [24]. The CDs with up-conversion performance can overcome the defects of the above-mentioned materials. Cao et al. firstly reported the up-conversion emission CDs and applied them for biological imaging, which can decrease photodamage of the cells and the tissues [25]. After that, CDs with up-conversion fluorescent properties have been focused on cell imaging, electrocatalysis and photocatalysis by researchers [26–29]. Only several researchers focused on applying down- and up-conversion CDs as fluorescence probes for detecting analytes [30–35]. These up-conversion CDs can achieve dual-signal sensing and improve detection accuracy, but their quantum yields are low and need to be improved, as well as the function needs to be further extended.

For glutathione (GSH), it is crucial to realize the real-time accurate detection of it in biological systems [36, 37]. Until now, it was scarcely found that the down- and up-conversion fluorescence of CDs for determining GSH. Herein, the CDs with down- and up-conversion fluorescence were obtained through hydrothermal heating of citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane. The CDs showed high quantum yields up to 62% under the optimized preparation conditions. Strikingly, it was demonstrated that the CDs can serve as bifunctional fluorescence nanoprobe to detect Hg2+ and GSH by down- and up-conversion through the “on-off-on” process (Scheme 1 ). Based on this phenomenon, these CDs were used to establish the logic gate. Finally, these CDs were employed for detection of Hg2+ and GSH in actual water samples with satisfactory results, which exhibited potential applications in biological samples.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (99%) and quinine sulfate (Tris, 95%) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). NaCl, KCl, CuSO4·5H2O, Hg(NO3)2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, MnSO4·H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, BaCO3, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3, AlCl3, CrCl3, As2O3 and Ga(NO3)3·6H2O were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GSH and other amino acids include: homocysteine, cystine, threonine, glycine, arginine, serine, histidine, phenylalanine, alanine, proline and tyrosine were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. All solution was prepared and dissolved by double-distilled water.

The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2011, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted by TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. The absorption spectra were measured on UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). Fluorescence spectra were measured by F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. Fluorescence lifetimes were recorded on an FLS980 (Edinburgh, UK).

The CDs were fabricated by hydrothermal process according to the previously reported method by our group with minor modification [6]. 0.96 g CA and 0.6 g Tris were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water with ultrasonic method for 10 min. The solution was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50 mL) preheated at 110 °C for 1 h and then heated at 220 °C for 5 h. Then the reactor was cooled to ambient temperature naturally. The formed deep-brown aqueous dispersion was centrifuged at medium speed (4000 rpm/min) for 15 min in order to remove insoluble particles. The CDs were further purified through dialysis tube (MW = 3500 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted reagent and small molecular species. The pure CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation (yield: 0.62 g) and were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and application.

The fluorescence quantum yield (φf) was determined by slope method through the reference of quinine sulfate (φf = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4): compared the integrated fluorescence intensity (λex = 330 nm) against absorbance of the CDs with that of the references. Then, φf was calculated by the equation [38]: φf = φst(Kf / Kst)(nf / nst)2, where “φ” is the fluorescence quantum yield, “K” is the slope of fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curves, “n” is the refractive index (=1.33 for water and 0.1 M H2SO4). The subscript “st” refers to quinine sulfate and “f” refers to the CDs. So, the equation was simplified to: φf = φst(Kf/Kst).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs With Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence Detection of Hg2+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and GSH was performed in HEPES buffer solution (pH 6.8) at room temperature. All the fluorescence measurements were recorded at excitation wavelength of 330 nm (using 5/5 nm slit width) and 660 nm (using 10/10 nm slit width), respectively.

To evaluate the sensitivity towards Hg2+, the CDs dispersion (0.02 mL 2 mg mL−1), about 9 mL HEPES and different volumes of Hg2+ stock solution (0.002 M) were added into 10 mL volumetric flask, and finally quantify to 10 mL. The selectivity for Hg2+ was confirmed by adding 0.2 mL single metal ion stock solution (0.002 M) including Hg2+and other metal ions in a similar way. The interference of other common metal ions towards Hg2+ was examined, by mixing 0.2 mL metal ions stock solutions (0.002 M) with 0.2 mL Hg2+ stock solutions (0.002 M) in a similar way. The fluorescence detection of Hg2+ was conducted by incubating above mixed solution for 30 min. To verify the dynamic quenching process of Hg2+ for the CDs, the fluorescence detection was carried out immediately after water bath for 30 min at a certain temperature.

To investigate the sensitivity towards GSH, the CDs dispersion (0.02 mL 2 mg mL−1), about 8 mL HEPES and 0.2 mL Hg2+ stock solution (0.002 M) were added into 10 mL volumetric flask. After incubated for 10 min, different volumes of GSH stock solution (0.002 M) were mixed with above mixture solution, and finally quantify to 10 mL. The selectivity for GSH was confirmed by adding 0.2 mL single amino acids stock solution (0.002 M) including GSH and other amino acids in a similar way. The fluorescence detection of GSH was finished by incubating above mixed solution for 60 min.

As shown in Scheme 1, the CDs were prepared via two-step heating hydrothermal treatment with CA and Tris. Typically, the Tris played a crucial role in shaping the structure of CDs due to its multiple hydroxyl and one amino group. In the first step, some oligomers had been formed when the intermolecular dehydration and hydrothermal polymerization happened, namely, the “polymerization” step. With the increase of temperature, carbonaceous nuclei grew gradually through the cross-linked polymerization. The heteroatoms, N and O, incorporated into the carbon matrix surfaces until the final CDs presented uniformly and isotropically, leading to the functionalization and excellent dispersion in water. The CDs were simply prepared via “polymerization” and “carbonization” steps with a high φf (62%).

TEM was used to analyze the morphology and structure of the obtained CDs. Fig. 1a showed that the CDs were spherical and dispersed without apparent aggregation. In addition, the CDs presented the diameters in the range of 2 to 6 nm and the average diameter was 3.75 nm, which agreed with Gaussian distribution (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (inset: Fig. 1a) exhibited the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which could be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene carbon.

To characterize the surface functional groups, the FT-IR spectrum was performed (Fig. 1c). The observed broad absorption bands at 3414 cm−1 and 3140 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH. The peak at 1647 cm−1 could be associated to amide bonding (CONH), whereas the peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1 were assigned to CN and CO stretching vibrations. The surface of CDs mainly contain –CONH, CN, CO bands and NH2, COH groups.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs presented one characteristic band at 330 nm (a: Fig. 1d), which was associated with the n-π* transitions of surface states. The charge density of the CDs can be changed by the electron-rich unsaturated nitrogen atoms, resulting in the electron transfer from π to π* of CDs, which can lead to strong fluorescence. Meanwhile, the maximum emission intensity (φf = 62%) was centered at 399 nm under a single excitation wavelength at 330 nm (b: Fig. 1d). The CDs aqueous solution showed pale-yellow and possessed obvious blue fluorescence emission when excited at 365 nm in UV-light (inset: Fig. 1d).

To investigate the optical properties of the CDs, the detailed fluorescence spectra of CDs were illustrated in Fig. 2a. The emission gradually shifted from 397 nm to 423 nm as excitation wavelength changed from 270 nm to 370 nm. The excitation-dependent emission of the CDs might be related to the surface state and particle size which was supported by TEM results [39]. The CDs displayed the strongest emission at 399 nm when excited at 330 nm. The related fluorescence φf was calculated to be 62%, it was beneficial for the CDs to acting as an excellent fluorescence probe. The high value of φf was attributed to the introduction of new intermediate states of energy, which was caused by the nitrogen atoms from the precursor Tris. Meanwhile, Fig. 2c showed the Commission International d'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate of CDs when excited from 270 nm to 370 nm. This result indicated that the down-conversion fluorescence was in the blue region.

Remarkably, as the excitation wavelength increased from 600 nm to 700 nm, the CDs exhibited frequency-doubling scattering spectra. Meanwhile, excitation-independent emission center at 398 nm was shown due to multiphoton processes (Fig. 2b). This process can be examined by constant energy difference of ~1.1 eV between the excitation light and emission light [40]:
(1)
E D
= c ∗ h /
λ ex
−
λ em
= 1.09
eV
where “E
D ” is an energy difference, “c” is the Lightspeed, “h” is the Planck constant, “λex” and “λem” is excitation wavelength and emission wavelength, respectively.

The E
D was obtained to be 1.09 eV that was consistent with the constant energy difference (approximately 1.1 eV), which indicated the occurrence of the anti-stokes process. However, unlike down-conversion fluorescence emission, the up-conversion was almost not shifted with excitation wavelength. Unlike the reported, the down- and up-conversion of CDs were independent of the excitation wavelength, which revealed that the fluorescence emission was associated to the excitation mode [30]. The electrons were excited and transferred to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital with high-energy state. Then excitons were relaxed back to orbital designation with lower energy state, resulting in up-conversion behavior. In addition, the CIE color coordinates of up-conversion of CDs converged at one point (Fig. 2d), which was consistent with the Fig. 2b results.

Because the pH of the medium is an important parameter, it can influence the fluorescence intensity and the efficiency of the quenching process. It was studied that the impact of pH for the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs increased as pH changing from 2.1 to 6.2, and slowly declined over 6.2. This anti-pH stability may be related to the abundant OH on the surface of CDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was almost unaffected over a broad range of NaCl concentrations from 0 to 1.0 mol/L (Fig. 3b). To observe the dispersibility and stability of CDs, the CDs were irradiated by helium‑neon gas laser with red light. (Fig. 3c). The Tyndall effect was presented clearly, and the 10 measurements were received constant signal value with 24 h as the interval. Thus, these results revealed that the CDs had excellent stability against pH variation and salt tolerance as well as good dispersibility and stability in aqueous solutions. These remarkable features can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsions between the CDs, their nanometer sizes, a large number of N and O groups on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Detection of Hg2+ by Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection of Hg2+ was measured after addition of various common metal cations (pH 6.8, HEPES buffer) under a single excitation wavelength at 330 and 660 nm, respectively. The selectivity of the CDs for Hg2+ was shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched by Hg2+ ions, while other metal ions such as Na+, K+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, As3+ and Ga3+showed quite few effect on the fluorescent intensity. It indicated that the CDs possessed excellent selectivity for Hg2+. To evaluate the sensitivity of the CDs to Hg2+, the fluorescence titration experiment was investigated. Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d showed that the down- and up-conversion fluorescence of the CDs gradually weakened with increasing of concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 40 μM under the excitation wavelength at 330 nm and 660 nm, respectively. The fluorescence quenching was exceeding 70%, it confirmed that the fluorescence was sensitive towards Hg2+. Moreover, the fluorescent quenching value showed linear detection range for Hg2+ in the range of 0–40 μM (inset: Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d), and the limit of detection was respectively determined to be 0.23 μM and 0.25 μM based on 3 × δblank/k (where δblank is the standard deviation of background (n = 12), k is the slope of the calibration curve). What's more, significant quenching occurred when Hg2+ was added to the other ions solution (Fig. 4e and f). This interference study also suggested that the CDs possessed high selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Detection of GSH by Down- and Up-conversion Fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Because Hg2+ has a strong affinity towards thiol-containing GSH, the CD-Hg2+ system can be used to detect GSH. As expected, upon the addition of GSH to CDs-Hg2+ system, over 90% emission intensity gradually restored with the increasing of the GSH concentration when excited at 330 and 660 nm, respectively (Fig. 5a and b). A linear relationship was given over the range of 0–30 μM with detection limits as low as 0.28 μM and 0.29 μM, respectively, that algorithm is the same as Hg2+ (n = 12). Moreover, to evaluate the selectivity of the CDs-Hg2+system for GSH, the fluorescence responses towards different amino acids were studied. As depicted in Fig. 5c and d, significant fluorescence enhancing was observed only thiol-containing compounds (cystine, homocysteine and GSH) via forming the stable HgS bond. Although cystine and homocysteine also restored the fluorescence, there are two reasons that CDs-Hg2+ system serves as an excellent sensing platform for detecting GSH by a dual signal. One is that the concentration of GSH (mM levels) is much higher than cystine and homocysteine (μM levels) in biological samples. On the other hand, cystine and homocysteine have achieved ultra-low concentration (nM levels) when the GSH concentration at μM levels [41, 42].

Therefore, the up-conversion detection and the down-conversion detection had the same linear range and close limit of detection. Compared with down-conversion detection, the up-conversion detection has larger stokes shift and this long wavelength excitation is more beneficial to the application of biological samples [43]. The combination of the two detection methods can achieve dual channel detection. This improves the accuracy for detection of Hg2+ and GSH.

To better elaborate the quenching mechanism, it was described that the Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of the CDs at various concentrations Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 6a, the Stern-Volmer plot of the down-conversion fitted a nonlinear equation within the concentration range of 0–40 μM, among which it was linear at 0 to 16 μM while displayed an upward deviation at higher concentrations. It suggested that fluorescence quenching was not caused by one mechanism [44]. The mechanisms of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect require the spectrum overlap between CDs and quencher [45, 46]. For Hg2+, there is no obvious absorption peak in the range of 300–500 nm. So, the quenching of the CDs are not related to FRET and inner filter effect. In addition, the low Hg2+concentration (0–16 μM) was described by the Stern-Volmer equation with a good linear behavior, and the Ksv of down-conversion was calculated to be 2.79 × 104 M−1, indicating that the quenching at low concentration was caused by static quenching [47–49].
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
where K
sv denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] presents the Hg2+ concentration, F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after addition of Hg2+, respectively.

To verify the occurrence of dynamic quenching mechanism, the decay curves for the CDs before and after adding different concentrations Hg2+ were performed as shown in Fig. 6b. The average lifetime of CDs remained almost unchanged upon addition of 10 μM Hg2+. However, when 30 μM Hg2+ was added, the average lifetime of CDs decreased from 13.96 ns to 10.00 ns. This result further confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Hg2+ was attributed to static and dynamic quenching mechanisms [50, 51].
In addition, the Stern-Volmer plot of the up-conversion was shown in Fig. S1, the regression equation also displayed local linear between 0 and 16 μM and the overall nonlinear between 0 and 40 μM.

This phenomenon is similar to that of down-conversion fluorescence. Thus, it suggested that down- and up conversion fluorescence is quenched by Hg2+ via the same mechanism, and the quenching mechanisms are attributed to both static and dynamic quenching.

Due to the significant fluorescence quenching and enhancement of the CDs, the logic gates were designed, such as “not”, “and” (Fig. 7 ). In this, the four possible input combinations (0, 0; 1, 0; 0, 1; 1, 1) were performed to obtain multiple logic gate. In this logic gate, the Hg2+ and GSH acted as input 1 and input 2, respectively, and the fluorescence signal acted as output. The logic gate “not” was achieved by giving Hg2+. The output of “0” indicated that the fluorescence was quenched in the presence of Hg2+. The “and” logic gate by using Hg2+ (fixed concentration) and GSH (varying concentration) was constructed. The output of “1” suggested that the fluorescence was recovered when Hg2+ and GSH presented at the same time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and GSH in Practical Water
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the feasibility of the CDs for practical applications, the spiked recovery experiments were measured for determination of Hg2+ and GSH levels in two water samples from tap water (Tianjin, China) and Pan Lake water (Tianjin, China). Two water samples were filtered first through 0.45 μM filters to remove large suspended particulates, and then spiked Hg2+ with concentrations of 5, 10 and 15 μM. Each concentration was measured three times. For the GSH assay, 20 μM Hg2+ was initially added to the both water samples and incubated for 30 min, then added GSH with concentrations of 5, 10 and 15 μM. The fluorescence test was conducted under the same condition. As illustrated in Tables 1 and 2
, the recovery values of Hg2+ were 96.2%–110.4% and GSH were 93.4%–96.9% by the developed method. It indicated that the CDs were applicable to analyze Hg2+ and GSH in real samples.

In addition, compared with other published methods, this proposed CDs exhibited higher quantum yields, larger linear ranges and could achieve dual channel detection. Thus, the CDs exhibited potential advantages for the determination of Hg2+ and GSH in real sample (Table 3 ).

In summary, it can be deduced that the CDs possess good down-and up-conversion fluorescence as well as φf of 62%. The study also reveals that the CDs can serve as a useful dual-channel fluorescent probe for Hg2+and GSH by down- and up-conversion emission. With addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity was quenched through dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanisms. Subsequently, the fluorescence of CDs-Hg2+ system was recovered with the addition of GSH due to the stronger affinity between GSH and Hg2+. A good linear relationship was displayed with the concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 40 μM with detection limit of 0.23 and 0.25 μM, respectively. The fluorescence recovery changed linearly with GSH concentrations in the range of 0–30 μM. The limits of detection were estimated to be 0.28 and 0.29 μM, respectively. Moreover, the CDs based molecular logic gates were designed, such as “Not” and “And”. When both Hg2+ and GSH were input, the gate active was constructed. Furthermore, the detection of Hg2+ and GSH in tap water and in lake water was researched and the recovery values of Hg2+ and GSH were calculated to be 96.2%–110.4% and 93.4%–96.9%, respectively. Thus, the CDs have great potential applications in environmental monitoring and biological imaging labeling agent.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. 1s
The Stern-Volmer plot of up-conversion for the quenching of CDs fluorescence at various concentrations of Hg2+; inset: Stern-Volmer plot of up-conversion at lower Hg2+ concentrations.
Fig. 1s
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.012.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21374078, 51678409), and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (Nos. 15JCYBJC18100, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507) and The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (No. 16PTSYJC00110).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots
Carbon dots as nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and l-cysteine by means of fluorescence “off–on” switching
A facile microwave-assisted fabrication of fluorescent carbon nitride quantum dots and their application in the detection of mercury ions
A ratiometric nanoprobe based on silver nanoclusters and carbon dots for the fluorescent detection of biothiols
Constructing a fluorescent probe for specific detection of catechol based on 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon dots
Carbon dots-bromoacetyl bromide conjugates as fluorescence probe for the detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
A sensitive turn on fluorescent probe for detection of biothiols using MnO2@ carbon dots nanocomposites
Quantum confinement and photoluminescence of well-aligned CdO nanofibers by a solvothermal route
A portable synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid based on inner filter effect
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Multifunctional nanoparticles: cost versus benefit of adding targeting and imaging capabilities
Synthesis and characterization studies of NiO nanorods for enhancing solar cell efficiency using photon upconversion materials
Light upconverting core–shell nanostructures: nanophotonic control for emerging applications
